content:
7 min read
1 hour ago

--

Open-sourcing a MaxMind DB writer for Rust, and the silent int32 corruption that only three independent readers could catch.
I wrote -5 into a database. It came back as 251.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

No error. No panic. No failing test. Every lookup returned a value, every value had the right type, and the file opened cleanly in every tool I threw at it. The data was just wrong. And it had been wrong, silently, in an encoder I’d been running in production for months.
This is the story of how that bug survived a green test suite, how it finally got caught, and what it took to turn an internal MaxMind DB encoder into something I was willing to put my name on publicly: mmdb-writer, now on crates.io.
From internal tool to public crate
A couple of months ago I wrote about building a MaxMind DB encoder in Rust to ship 1.37 million BGP prefixes in 8.9 MiB. That encoder was an internal tool: it lived inside a fraud-prevention pipeline, wrote the exact shapes of data that pipeline produced, and was tested against the exact lookups that pipeline made. Within those walls it was solid.
The Rust ecosystem still didn’t have a general-purpose MMDB writer, though. There’s a great reader, maxminddb, and the standard advice for writing was still “shell out to Go’s mmdbwriter.” So I decided to build the crate properly, from scratch: full parity with the official Go writer. IPv4 and IPv6 trees, tree aliasing, merge strategies, range inserts, reserved-network handling, serde support, the whole surface.
Porting the core took a while. Hardening it for strangers is where it got interesting, because strangers don’t use your data shapes. They use all of them.
The bug: one wrong mental model about int32
MMDB encodes integers with their leading zero bytes trimmed. A uint32 holding 5 doesn’t take four payload bytes on disk; it takes one. The control byte carries the actual length, and the reader pads the value back out. This is a big part of why the format is so compact.
My encoder applied the same logic to int32: trim the redundant leading bytes, let the reader reconstruct the value. For -5, which is 0xFFFFFFFB in two’s complement, the “redundant” leading bytes are 0xFF, so it wrote a single byte:
0x01 0x01 0xFB # size=1, type=int32, payload = FBThat’s perfectly valid MMDB. The problem is what readers do with it. Here is the official Python reader’s int32 decoder (maxminddb 3.1.1, lightly abridged):
def _decode_int32(self, size, offset):
packed_bytes = self._buffer[offset : offset + size]
if size != 4:
packed_bytes = packed_bytes.rjust(4, b"\x00")
(value,) = struct.unpack(b"!i", packed_bytes)
return valuerjust(4, b"\x00"). Readers zero-extend short payloads. They do not sign-extend. There is no rule anywhere in the spec that says “short int32 payloads are sign-extended”. I had invented that rule myself, because it’s how the trimming ought to work if you designed it around signed values. It isn’t designed around signed values.
So 0xFB, zero-extended, becomes 0x000000FB = 251. And -300 (0xFFFFFED4, trimmed to two bytes) becomes 0x0000FED4 = 65,236. Every negative int32 my encoder had ever written was garbage. Every non-negative one was perfect.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why a green suite missed it
The internal encoder had round-trip tests: write a database, read it back with the official maxminddb reader, compare. They all passed, for an embarrassingly simple reason: none of the test fixtures contained a negative number. IP intelligence data is ASNs, port counts, timestamps, country IDs. It’s unsigned all the way down. The one signed type in the format was exercised only with values that happened to encode identically under both my wrong rule and the real one.
That’s the uncomfortable property of round-trip tests: they verify your writer against your choice of inputs, and your choice of inputs comes from the same mental model as your encoder. If the model has a hole, the fixtures have the same hole.
What finally caught it was a test written out of paranoia rather than suspicion. While hardening the serde layer for release, I added a “type surface” test: one database record containing every MMDB type at its extremes: u16::MAX, u32::MAX, u64::MAX, u128::MAX, i32::MIN, zero, -5, -300, 500-byte strings, nested maps, raw bytes. All of it written through the serde path and read back through the official reader.
assertion failed: `-5 == 251`Months of production traffic, dozens of green test runs, and the bug fell to the first test that ever asked the right question.
The fix is one cast
The correct rule, which is also what the official Go writer implements: treat the int32’s bit pattern as unsigned, then trim leading zero bytes. Positive values still shrink. Negative values keep their sign bytes and always occupy four bytes.
fn encode_i32_trimmed(buf: &mut Vec<u8>, value: i32) -> usize {
#[allow(clippy::cast_sign_loss)]
encode_uint_trimmed(buf, u128::from(value as u32), 4)
}So -5 now writes as:
0x04 0x01 0xFF 0xFF 0xFF 0xFB # size=4, type=int32, full widthOne cast. The diff is a handful of characters. Finding it was the entire job.
One reader is an oracle. Three readers are a jury.
The deeper lesson wasn’t “add more tests.” It was that a writer verified against one reader is verified against one implementation, and implementations forgive. If the reader I picked had happened to sign-extend, my bug would have been invisible forever, until someone opened the file with a different reader.
So the release gate became a jury. I generate a matrix of seven databases that collectively exercise every feature of the crate: IPv4-only trees, IPv6 with aliasing, merge strategies, range inserts, holes punched with insert_with, reserved-network carve-outs, metadata options, every data type at its limits, a 10,000-network database with deduplication, a write→load→extend→write round trip. Every one of them has to decode identically under:

• the official Rust reader (maxminddb 0.29),

• the official Python reader (maxminddb 3.1.1),

• MaxMind’s own Go-based mmdbinspect.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Three codebases, three languages, three independent sets of assumptions. For a binary format, agreement across independent readers is the closest thing you get to ground truth.
I also ran the numbers against the official Go writer directly: building the same 100,000-network database, mmdb-writer and Go’s mmdbwriter produce output within 3 bytes of each other (1,735,497 vs 1,735,494; the delta is metadata fields), in a statistical tie on wall-clock time. When two writers born years apart in different languages agree to the third decimal place on file size, both trees are almost certainly right.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Mutation testing, or: how I crashed my own Mac
Round-trip tests now covered the values. But who tests the tests? For that I ran cargo-mutants, which mutates the source: it flips a > to a <, swaps a + for a -, deletes a branch, recompiles, and checks whether any test notices. Every mutant that survives is a gap: a line of code whose behavior no test actually pins down.
Two things came out of this. The first was a set of genuinely missing assertions: byte-exact checks for pointer encodings at every size-class boundary, the 28-bit record’s nibble packing, control-byte edges. Each survivor either got a killer test or a proof that the mutation was equivalent (several were | → ^ on bit masks that provably never overlap).
The second thing was a hard lesson in what mutation testing does to a tree library. One mutant flipped a comparison in the network-enumeration walk, which turned “iterate the subnets of this prefix” into “iterate approximately 2119 subnets.” The test didn’t fail; it started allocating. cargo-mutants was running several mutants in parallel, more than one of them found a similar infinite path, and by the time I looked up from another terminal, the machine had committed ~90 GB of memory and died.
The fix was boring and effective: one mutant at a time, plus a watchdog that kills any test process whose resident set crosses 4 GB. Final tally on the focused run: 168 mutants, 137 killed by tests, 9 timed out (loops the timeout catches), 10 survivors, all ten provably equivalent mutations. If you maintain anything that walks a data structure, cap the memory before you let a tool deliberately break your loop conditions. Ask me how I know.
What shipped
mmdb-writer 0.1.0 is on crates.io, MIT OR Apache-2.0:

• Full-parity feature set with Go’s official mmdbwriter: IPv4/IPv6 trees, IPv4 aliasing (IPv4-mapped, 6to4, Teredo), replace/merge/read-modify-write insert strategies, arbitrary IP-range inserts, reserved-network handling, automatic 24/28/32-bit record sizing, pointer deduplication.

• #![forbid(unsafe_code)], three required dependencies, MSRV 1.85.

• serde by default, writer.insert(network, &any_serialize_type), with a no-serde Value API underneath, and a load feature to open and extend existing .mmdb files.

• Deterministic output: same inputs plus a pinned build epoch give you byte-identical files, which makes the whole three-reader verification story reproducible.
The int32 bug never shipped in the crate: it was caught in release hardening, before v0.1.0 existed. The internal encoder it descended from wasn’t so lucky, and that’s exactly the point: the difference between the two is not the code, it’s the verification harness around it.
If you maintain a writer for any binary format, the checklist that would have saved me months of silently wrong bytes is short: test every type at both extremes, verify against every independent reader you can find, and let a mutation tool tell you which of your tests are decorative. Green is a color, not a proof.
The crate is at github.com/SergioKingOne/mmdb-writer, docs at docs.rs/mmdb-writer. It’s my first open-source project. Issues and PRs are very welcome, especially from anyone who opens my files with a reader I haven’t met yet.
